docData.title: Patient Rights and Responsibilities
docData.subtitle: Respect, informed choice, privacy, safety, and advocacy in every care setting
docData.description: A practical course that helps healthcare personnel recognize, protect, document, and escalate patient-rights concerns. Content is educational and must be applied with current organizational policy and applicable federal and state requirements.

blocks.title: Every patient is a person first
blocks.bodyHtml: Patient rights begin with dignity. Care must respect the patient's identity, values, beliefs, privacy, preferences, and personal goals without discrimination or retaliation.

blocks.title: Core protections
blocks.introHtml: Open each item to review how these protections appear in daily care.

items.title: Respectful treatment
items.bodyHtml: Use the patient's preferred name and respectful language. Protect modesty and explain care before touching or repositioning the patient.

items.title: Non-discrimination
items.bodyHtml: Care decisions must not be reduced or delayed because of race, color, national origin, religion, sex, age, disability, sexual orientation, gender identity, diagnosis, payment source, or another protected characteristic.

items.title: Cultural and spiritual preferences
items.bodyHtml: Ask rather than assume. Accommodate cultural, spiritual, and personal practices when they can be supported safely.

blocks.question: Which action best demonstrates respect for patient dignity?

choices.text: Discuss the patient's condition in a public hallway
Explain the procedure, protect privacy, and ask permission before beginning
Use a nickname without asking because it is faster

blocks.html:
Patients have the right to receive information in a way they can understand. Use plain language, qualified language assistance when needed, accessible formats, and communication aids. Do not rely on a child or unqualified companion to interpret clinical information except where an emergency policy specifically permits it.

Use teach-back: ask the patient to explain the plan in their own words. Teach-back checks the clarity of the explanation; it is not a test of the patient.
Practice point: Document the language or accessibility service used and any remaining communication barrier requiring follow-up.

blocks.question: A patient has limited English proficiency. What is the safest routine response?

choices.text: Use a qualified interpreter and speak directly to the patient
Ask another patient to translate
Skip the explanation if the procedure is common

lessons.title: Informed Consent and the Right to Refuse

blocks.text: Consent is a process, not only a signature

cards.title: Clear disclosure
cards.teaser: What does the patient need to know?
cards.bodyHtml: Explain the purpose, expected benefits, material risks, reasonable alternatives, and likely consequences of declining, within the responsibilities of your role.

cards.title: Understanding
cards.bodyHtml: Invite questions and use teach-back. Address communication, cognitive, sensory, and health-literacy needs.

cards.title: Voluntary decision
cards.bodyHtml: The choice must be free from coercion. A capable adult may refuse care even when the team disagrees with the decision.

cards.title: Documentation and escalation
cards.bodyHtml: Document the discussion and decision according to policy. Escalate concerns about capacity, emergency authority, or disagreement to the appropriate clinician or supervisor.

blocks.html: Never: threaten, shame, mislead, or withhold routine care because a patient asks questions or refuses a recommended intervention.

blocks.question: A capable adult refuses a recommended dressing change after risks and alternatives are explained. What should the team do?

choices.text: Respect the refusal, document it, notify the responsible clinician, and continue appropriate care
Proceed because the intervention is clinically recommended
Discharge the patient immediately for noncompliance

blocks.html: Patients may communicate future care preferences through advance directives and may identify an authorized representative. Staff must follow the organization's process for locating, validating, documenting, and communicating these instructions.

blocks.title: When questions arise
blocks.introHtml: Use the established chain of command rather than making legal assumptions.

items.title: Decision-making capacity is questioned
items.bodyHtml: Notify the responsible licensed clinician. Capacity is decision-specific and must be evaluated by an authorized professional under policy.

items.title: Family members disagree
items.bodyHtml: Maintain safety, identify the legally authorized decision-maker, and escalate through clinical leadership, ethics, risk, or legal resources as policy directs.

items.title: A directive cannot be located
items.bodyHtml: Document the concern and promptly notify the responsible clinician. Do not invent or infer instructions from incomplete information.

blocks.question: What is the correct response when family members disagree about a patient's documented wishes?

choices.text: Choose the family member who speaks most confidently
Follow the chain of command and involve the authorized clinical and organizational resources
Ignore the directive until the family reaches agreement

blocks.text: Access only what your work requires

blocks.html: Protect visual, verbal, written, and electronic information. Access records only for an authorized work purpose, verify recipients, use approved communication systems, secure screens and documents, and discuss care only in appropriate settings.

If information is sent to the wrong person, a device is lost, or unauthorized access is suspected, preserve evidence and report the event immediately through the privacy or incident process. Do not attempt to hide or independently resolve the breach.

choices.text: Open a relative's chart because you are concerned
Verify the recipient and use an approved secure channel
Photograph a record on a personal phone for convenience

lessons.title: Safe Care, Freedom from Abuse, and Least Restrictive Practice

blocks.html: Patients have the right to care in an environment that protects them from abuse, neglect, exploitation, harassment, unnecessary restraint, and avoidable harm. Restrictions must never be used for convenience, punishment, retaliation, or staffing shortages.

blocks.title: Responding to a safety concern
blocks.introHtml: Follow these steps within your role and organizational policy.

cards.title: 1. Protect immediate safety
cards.bodyHtml: Call emergency assistance or follow the immediate safety protocol when the patient or another person is at imminent risk.

cards.title: 2. Report promptly
cards.bodyHtml: Notify the supervisor and required safeguarding, clinical, compliance, or external reporting resources without delaying for personal investigation.

cards.title: 3. Document objectively
cards.bodyHtml: Record direct observations, the patient's own words when relevant, actions taken, and notifications made. Avoid conclusions outside your role.

blocks.question: A patient says a caregiver struck them. What is the best initial response?

choices.text: Ensure immediate safety, listen without leading, and activate the required reporting process
Confront the caregiver alone before telling anyone
Wait for visible injury before documenting the concern

lessons.title: Complaints, Grievances, and Freedom from Retaliation

blocks.text: A complaint is an opportunity to protect the patient

blocks.html: Patients and representatives may voice concerns, request review, and use grievance processes without retaliation or disruption of appropriate care. Staff should listen, address immediate needs, explain the next step, and route the concern to the responsible department.
Recommended language: “Thank you for telling me. I will document your concern, address any immediate safety issue, and connect you with the person responsible for follow-up.”

blocks.question: A patient complains about delayed care. Which response is appropriate?

choices.text: Tell the patient complaints may affect future services
Listen, address urgent needs, explain the process, and route the concern
Refuse to document a verbal complaint

lessons.title: Integrated Practice and Final Knowledge Check

blocks.title: Final knowledge check
blocks.bodyHtml: Apply the patient-rights principles from this course. A passing score of 80% is required. Review organizational policy whenever a situation exceeds your role or authority.

blocks.question: A patient asks for an interpreter, refuses one treatment, and wants to file a complaint. Which response protects all three rights?

choices.text: Arrange qualified language assistance, support informed refusal, and explain the complaint process without retaliation
Delay the interpreter until the patient accepts treatment
Ask the family to decide whether the complaint is necessary

blocks.question: Which actions are appropriate when a patient-rights concern is identified? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Address immediate safety needs
Document objective facts and actions taken
Use the designated escalation or reporting process
Hide the event if no injury occurred

blocks.html: Completion commitment: Protect dignity, explain choices, verify understanding, respect lawful decisions, safeguard information, respond to danger, document objectively, and escalate promptly.

disclosures: No commercial support or product endorsement.
This course provides general education and does not replace organizational policy or legal advice.
requirements: Review the current patient-rights and grievance policies for your organization
Complete all required lessons and knowledge checks
docData.accomodations: Contact the training administrator for language assistance, captions, screen-reader compatible material, or another reasonable accommodation.
